entities.name: apabhraṃśa
entities.cite: apabhramsas prasiddhim agatah — have attained establishment

entities.name: sādhurūpa
entities.cite: for those speakers, sadhur avacakah — it is the *correct* form that fails to express

entities.name: prasiddhī
entities.cite: become the *current* forms, rudhi (1.128) now running entirely on the fallen side

entities.name: viguṇasamudāya
entities.cite: among vigunesu abhidhatrsu, speakers gone defective

entities.name: vācakatva
entities.cite: expressiveness follows establishment, and establishment follows transmission, wherever transmission goes

entities.name: adrṣṭa
entities.cite: the one ground (adrsta) where observation cannot follow to contest it

entities.name: pāramparyā
entities.cite: Paramparyat: by transmission, generation upon generation

axioms.name: prasiddhim_agatah
axioms.cite: apabhramsas prasiddhim agatah — have attained establishment, become the *current* forms

axioms.name: asraya_vigunasamudaya
axioms.cite: among vigunesu abhidhatrsu, speakers gone defective (communities severed from the sista line)

axioms.name: avacakah
axioms.cite: for those speakers, sadhur avacakah — it is the *correct* form that fails to express

axioms.name: vacakatva_niyamita_prasiddhya
axioms.cite: expressiveness follows establishment, and establishment follows transmission, wherever transmission goes

axioms.name: sadhutva_amangala
axioms.cite: sadhutva confers no magical reach beyond the community that sustains it

axioms.name: adrsta_rakshana
axioms.cite: What survives the concession is only, but exactly, the dharma-claim (1.27, 1.139): the established corrupt form expresses, in its own domain, as well as Sanskrit ever did — what it does not do is bear the unseen fruit

denials.name: sadhurupa_atma_vacak
denials.cite: sadhutva confers no magical reach beyond the community that sustains it

denials.name: parampara_sadhut_rakshan
denials.cite: The relay of 1.152 has inverted: where gavi is what everyone says and gauh is heard as strange noise, the mediation runs the other way or not at all

claims.name: avacakah
claims.cite: it is the correct form that is non-expressive

rejected_readings.label: intrinsic_correctness
rejected_readings.rendering: The correct form remains expressive and authoritative regardless of speaker community.
rejected_readings.why: Violates the core doctrine that expressiveness follows establishment and community. The verse teaches that correctness (sadhutva) confers no reach beyond the community—this reading inappropriately grants the correct form trans-community authority that the commentary explicitly denies.

rejected_readings.label: ignorance_model
rejected_readings.rendering: Defective speakers simply fail to recognize the superiority of the correct form due to ignorance.
rejected_readings.why: Treats 1.153 as a normative lament about speaker failure, when the commentary insists on a descriptive reading that grants the Prakrits full semantic citizenship within their communities. This misses the fundamental inversion: it is not that the correct form is truly expressive but unknown; rather, in those communities, the established corrupt form *is* the expressive form.

rejected_readings.label: transmission_preserves
rejected_readings.rendering: Transmission through established lineages preserves the correct form and its authority.
rejected_readings.why: Contradicts the verse's explicit teaching that transmission can degrade forms (when it runs through broken lineages), and that the established form—even if corrupt—is what is expressive. The commentary shows transmission as value-neutral: it carries what it carries, and expressiveness follows what is carried, not what should have been carried.

rejected_readings.label: normative_retreat
rejected_readings.rendering: Correctness and establishment are ultimately reconciled by a hidden normative standard that applies universally.
rejected_readings.why: Misses the intentional concession: the verse shows that the normative claim survives *only* in the realm of the unseen (adrsta), beyond observation. Any attempt to reconcile correctness and presiding usage within the observable domain violates the verse's retreat to this single unseen ground.

notes: Strain: the doctrine is purely relational—no intrinsic essences, only establishment and expressiveness relative to communities. The six sorts (absolute, power, manifestation, property, linguistic-item, cognition) capture the anatomy, but the verse's radical claim is that expressiveness is not intrinsic to the correct form but determined by prasiddhī and community. The only refuge for normativity is adrsta (unseen fruit), which lies outside the observable domain entirely. This is unusually honest for a grammarian: language works how language communities work; correctness becomes a claim about consequences (the unseen fruit) rather than present fact.
